blueprint_refs: Motivation for a target day is computed from the past 7 observed days (not necessarily consecutive calendar days) prior to the target date within 2021 only.
validation targets ... ONLY keep validation dates d where the user has at least 7 prior observed days ... for Motivation computation

evidence.metrics: Validation metrics show ToD mismatch and stop-count errors (tod_jsd_avg=0.0511, stop_count_abs_mean_error=1.2907). Code computes motivation even when <7 prior days exist.

motivation-window-not-enforced-7-prior-d.error_identification: Motivation is intended to be computed from exactly the past 7 observed days (when available), but the simulator uses whatever number of prior days exist (1–7) and silently falls back when none exist.
motivation-window-not-enforced-7-prior-d.root_cause_analysis: MobilitySimulator._compute_motivation() takes prior_days[-7:] without asserting len(prior_days) >= 7 and returns non-neutral motivation for <7 days. Blueprint requires 7-day context for target-day conditioning; while validation split enforces >=7 for included targets, this function is also used during calibration rollouts and could condition on shorter histories if invoked outside the validated target set.
motivation-window-not-enforced-7-prior-d.correct_approach: Enforce blueprint semantics in _compute_motivation(): if len(prior_days) < 7 return neutral motivation (multipliers/deltas = 0/1). Additionally, in rollout/generate_day, optionally assert that the passed-in target day meets the >=7 prior-days condition when simulating validation, to catch leakage/misuse early.
motivation-window-not-enforced-7-prior-d.key_insight: If a feature is defined on a fixed-length history window, enforce the window length in code (assert or neutral fallback), otherwise you get distribution shift between calibration/validation and unstable behavior.

blueprint_refs: stops-per-day distribution ... Base rate is then modulated by Motivation computed from the past 7 days
policy: sample baseline number of stops ... from learned per-user distributions
stop-count-model-clamped-adjustment-caus.error_identification: Simulated days have noticeably incorrect number-of-stops distribution and mean, despite fitting per-user stop_count_hist.
stop-count-model-clamped-adjustment-caus.root_cause_analysis: _sample_stop_count() samples a base stop count from an empirical histogram but then applies an ad-hoc, capped adjustment: at most 3 integer steps plus a fractional step. This can systematically under/over-shoot the motivation-scaled target, especially for users whose empirical stop counts have wider support. In generate_day(), the loop may further truncate remaining_steps based on time budget, creating additional bias vs the sampled stop budget.
stop-count-model-clamped-adjustment-caus.correct_approach: Replace the capped adjustment with a proper reweighting of the discrete histogram: construct a PMF over counts where log-prob is log(hist[count]) + lambda * count (or similar) to match the desired mean implied by stop_multiplier, then sample from that PMF. Also incorporate feasibility (time budget) by sampling a feasible count conditional on start time (e.g., reject-sample counts that exceed max_additional).
stop-count-model-clamped-adjustment-caus.key_insight: When you need to modulate a discrete empirical distribution, reweight the whole distribution (exponential tilting) instead of post-hoc clamped nudges; clamping creates biased means and KL spikes.

blueprint_refs: trip_distance_wasserstein: join lat/lon via poi_category_192021_longitude_latitude.json; compute haversine distance between consecutive stops

evidence.metrics: trip_distance_wasserstein=1.6615; coord_match_rate gt=0.3010 vs sim=0.2687; stdout: 'added 3858 trajectory-only POIs without coordinates'.

trip-distance-metric-biased-by-missing-c.error_identification: Trip-distance distribution is still materially off, and the simulated coordinate-match rate is lower than ground truth, which changes which trips are even included in the metric computation.
trip-distance-metric-biased-by-missing-c.root_cause_analysis: Many observed POIs are added with lat/lon=None (load_data). Evaluator._trip_distances only includes legs where both endpoints have coordinates, so the evaluated distribution is conditional on coord-available trips. The simulator adds only a small coord_bonus (0.12) and defaults unknown distances to 2.0km in _travel_minutes, but it can still generate more coord-missing legs than the ground truth subset, changing both the sample set and the distance distribution. This is a metric/selection bias problem, not just a distance-penalty tuning problem.
trip-distance-metric-biased-by-missing-c.correct_approach: Make coordinate availability an explicit part of candidate selection to match the metric’s conditioning: when current_poi has coords (or anchor has coords), restrict candidates to coord-known POIs with high probability (e.g., 0.8–0.95), falling back to coord-unknown only when category pool is empty. Separately, report and optimize against coord_match_rate internally (as a diagnostic, not a new required metric) to ensure the evaluated sample is comparable.
trip-distance-metric-biased-by-missing-c.key_insight: If evaluation filters to coord-available legs, the simulator must match the same conditioning set; otherwise you are optimizing distance on a moving target.

blueprint_refs: Parse observed day-strings into structured trajectories (date, ordered visits with POI category, POI id, time).
timestamps are rounded to nearest minute for fitting and generation.

evidence.metrics: tod_jsd_avg=0.0511; sample outputs show many starts at 00:xx. parse_day_record stops parsing on first decreasing timestamp, potentially discarding late-day visits.

overnight-wrap-parsing-truncates-traject.error_identification: Time-of-day fit remains imperfect and some generated days start extremely early (00:xx), indicating the learned start-time distribution may be contaminated; additionally, truncating parsed trajectories can distort stop counts and transitions.
overnight-wrap-parsing-truncates-traject.root_cause_analysis: parse_day_record() interprets any within-record time decrease as an overnight wrap and breaks, discarding remaining visits. If the raw data contains small out-of-order timestamps (not true midnight crossings), this truncation removes visits, shifting the first-visit time earlier and reducing per-day stop counts used for training statistics (start_time_hist, stop_count_hist, transition_pmf).
overnight-wrap-parsing-truncates-traject.correct_approach: Differentiate true midnight wrap from small ordering noise: only treat as overnight wrap if the decrease is large (e.g., previous >= 20:00 and next <= 04:00) or if a date boundary token exists (none here). Otherwise, skip the offending visit (continue) or clamp it to last_t+1 minute, and keep parsing. Log counts of (a) minor decreases skipped, (b) major decreases treated as wrap, to verify behavior on 1921Y.json.
overnight-wrap-parsing-truncates-traject.key_insight: When parsing sequential time data, ‘monotone’ violations need a tolerance model; breaking on first violation silently corrupts downstream distributions.

blueprint_refs: encounter-driven category awareness ... additive term to the category utility

evidence.metrics: transition_divergence=0.0294 (good but not best), category_share_mae=0.0119 (good). Code applies awareness keyed by coarse category but multiplies probabilities for fine category tokens, potentially inconsistent across mapping.

category-choice-mixes-fine-and-coarse-in.error_identification: Social influence is intended to operate at coarse category level, but the model’s category sampling operates on fine categories while applying awareness via coarse mapping, which can unevenly amplify some fine categories depending on mapping coverage.
category-choice-mixes-fine-and-coarse-in.root_cause_analysis: In rollout(), awareness is stored on coarse categories. In _choose_next_category(), it computes coarse=to_coarse_category(c, ...) for each fine category c and uses aw from coarse. This causes all fine categories mapping to the same coarse to be boosted equally even if the time profile/transition pmf are fine-grained; additionally, fine categories not present in catto.json may never receive awareness boosts, creating inconsistent social effects.
category-choice-mixes-fine-and-coarse-in.correct_approach: Decide one resolution for category choice: either (a) sample in coarse space consistently (convert transition/time profiles to coarse at training time), or (b) keep fine sampling but store awareness in fine space (no mapping) and only map to coarse for evaluation. If keeping coarse awareness, distribute coarse awareness back to fine categories proportionally to their baseline probabilities within the coarse bucket.
category-choice-mixes-fine-and-coarse-in.key_insight: Do not mix aggregation levels inside a probabilistic choice model unless you explicitly define how mass transfers between levels; otherwise you introduce hidden, data-dependent biases.

blueprint_refs: Calibrate ... on the 2021 training period ... Validate on holdout dates (2021 only) and report goodness-of-fit metrics.

evidence.metrics: Best objective is selected on a 250-day training subsample; validation objective differs (training best_obj=3.2770 vs validation objective=2.8357).

calibration-objective-uses-training-only.error_identification: The calibration procedure is noisy and may pick parameters that fit a small subsample rather than generalizing, contributing to residual validation errors (notably stop count and trip distance).
calibration-objective-uses-training-only.root_cause_analysis: RandomSearchCalibrator.fit() evaluates each parameter set on a single deterministic subsample of 250 training days and uses a single rollout (no repeated seeds, no cross-validation within training). Given stochastic simulation and heterogeneity across users/days, the objective has high variance, so the chosen ‘best’ can be suboptimal on full validation.
calibration-objective-uses-training-only.correct_approach: Reduce objective variance: evaluate each proposed parameter set on multiple subsamples (or bootstrap) and average objective, or increase calibration_day_budget. Also reset agents’ transient category_awareness before each evaluation rollout to avoid cross-iteration contamination of the objective (currently awareness persists across rollouts inside the calibration loop).
calibration-objective-uses-training-only.key_insight: Stochastic simulators need low-variance calibration signals (multiple rollouts / resets / larger samples); otherwise random search overfits noise.
